title: HavenRx production regressions after model v2.3 quantization
preview:
From: Laura Mendez <laura.mendez@havenrx.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Mike Johnson <mike.johnson@havenrx.com>, sre@havenrx.com
Date: Mon, 14 Jun 2026 08:12:04 -0700
Subject: Production: sharp latency + error spike after model update

Ava —

We're seeing a serious degradation in production since the model upgrade rolled on Friday night (redwood model v2.3 with the new quantization profile). Key impacts:
- p50 unchanged, but p99 latency jumped from ~420ms to ~1.8s over the weekend
- error rate (5xx) rose to ~4.8% on certain heavy-traffic endpoints
- throughput dropped ~22% under sustained load; timeouts reported by our downstream processing

This is live traffic for our med-prioritization flow — it's directly impacting response SLAs and our internal handling. We need an immediate plan: rollback, mitigation, or hotfix. Our SRE can open a call if helpful.

Please treat as high priority.

— Laura
Laura Mendez
Sr. Engineering Manager, Platform
HavenRx

From: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 14 Jun 2026 08:45:33 -0700
Subject: Re: Production: sharp latency + error spike after model update (technical notes)

Folks — quick triage summary from the runtime team.

What we observed: the v2.3 model with the new quantization profile (q3->q4 mixed path) introduces a different decode kernel path that increases per-token CPU/GPU synchronization under the beam/ sampling config used by HavenRx. Under typical sequences the KV cache expands differently and memory paging between CUDA memory + host becomes more frequent at the tail latencies.

Measured from Canary -> Prod telemetry:
- baseline (v2.2, q3_profile): p99 ~420ms, error ~0.3%
- post-upgrade (v2.3, q4_mixed): p99 ~1.8s, 5xx errors to 4.8% concentrated on requests >512 tokens
- throughput per GPU decreased ~18-25% depending on batch mixing

Root cause hypothesis: the quantization selection pushed some attention kernels to a slower fallback that greatly impacts longer sequences. There are also memory fragmentation effects that cause transient OOMs->5xx.

Immediate remediation options (ranked by speed of mitigation):
A) Roll back to v2.2 (last known good) — fastest, <30m to flip and validate if we have hot spares and the model artifacts cached.
B) Adjust quantization profile to q4_light (tighter bias toward q3 path) — medium effort, ~2-4 hours to build/test and canary.
C) Apply request-side throttling / drop long-sequence sampling for the next 2 hours — quick but degrades quality for those inputs.

Attachments: p99_latency_report_20260614.csv (aggregated telemetry), error_trace_645.log (sample 5xx trace).

Recommendation: Start with rollback (A) to stabilize production, then implement B as the long-term fix with canary. I'll prepare a rollback playbook (rollback_playbook.md) and target a rollback window once we get sign-off.

— David
David Kim
Principal Software Engineer, Serving Runtime
Redwood Inference

source.subject: SIG: KV-cache retention & model training commitments
messages:
From: Brad Keller <brad.keller@medisecure.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: MediSecure Procurement <procurement@medisecure.com>
Date: Thu, 20 May 2026 10:12:00 -0700
Subject: SIG follow-up: KV cache + training question

Hi Aisha,

Thanks for the SIG answers you provided earlier — our security team has one last set of clarifying questions before we close the questionnaire: specifically about the inference KV cache and retention/usage of request-response logs for model training. Could you confirm:  

- Whether the KV cache (model key/value state used during inference) is ever written to durable storage or logs, and if so what the retention window is.
- The retention window for request/response logs (prompts and model outputs) for your hosted API by default, and whether that can be configured to 0 for managed enterprise deployments.
- Whether customer prompt/response content is ever used to further train or fine-tune models without an explicit opt-in.

We need a short, procurement-ready statement we can attach to our SIG response (one paragraph + any relevant artifacts). Also let us know whether you can provide an exported evidence PDF or SOC 2 control mapping for our records.

Appreciate the quick help — we are on a tight procurement timeline.

Thanks,
Brad
Brad Keller
Senior InfoSec Analyst, MediSecure Health
security@medisecure.com

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Brad Keller <brad.keller@medisecure.com>
Date: Fri, 21 May 2026 09:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SIG follow-up: KV cache + training question

Hi Brad — thanks for the clear list. Below is a concise, procurement-friendly statement followed by the technical details. I attached a short PDF with the same wording plus a SOC 2 controls summary.

Procurement statement (one-paragraph):
Redwood Inference treats customer request/response content and inference state as customer data. By default, hosted API request/response logs are retained for 30 days to support debugging, abuse detection, and service reliability; customers on Redwood Dedicated or Private deployments may configure log retention to meet contractual requirements, including zero retention. The KV cache used during inference is an in-memory/session state and is not persisted to long-term storage as part of normal operation. Redwood does not use customer prompt or response content to train or fine-tune models without explicit, opt-in written consent. Audit access to customer data is tightly controlled, logged, and governed by our SOC 2 controls.

Technical details / clarifications:
- KV cache:  
  - The KV cache (attention keys/values and ephemeral decoding state) exists in RAM on the serving node for the duration of the session. It may be serialized in memory for intra-cluster transfer during scaling operations but is not written to durable customer-accessible object stores as part of normal inference. Redwood engineers do not persist KV cache to long-term storage except under rare, documented incident response workflows (and those actions are logged and subject to approval).
- Prompt/response logs (hosted):  
  - Default retention: 30 days. Purpose: debugging, abuse detection, quality monitoring.
  - Enterprise/Dedicated/Private: retention is configurable in contract; default for Dedicated can be set to match customer requirements including 0 days (no retention).
- Training on customer data:  
  - Redwood does not use customer prompt/response content to train or update model weights unless the customer provides explicit opt-in via a written agreement. Aggregated telemetry and non-identifiable operational metrics may be used to improve platform reliability but are not traceable to individual prompt content.
- Access controls and logging:  
  - Access to raw request/response data is strictly limited to authorized roles (SRE and Support under break-glass with approvals). All such access is logged and reviewed as part of SOC 2 change and access control processes.

If you need a short signed attestation line for procurement ("Redwood attests that..."), let me know and I can coordinate with Legal to provide a dated statement.

Attached: redwood-sig-answers-20260522.pdf (procurement statement + control mapping), soc2-controls-summary-2026.pdf

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
support@redwood.com

From: Brad Keller <brad.keller@medisecure.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 21 May 2026 14:15:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SIG follow-up: KV cache + training question

Aisha — this is great, thank you. Two quick asks before we mark the SIG row as closed:  

1) Procurement wants a single-line attestation we can drop into our vendor register (e.g., "Redwood attests that customer data is not used to train models without explicit consent"). Can Legal sign/date that?
2) Can you confirm whether audit logs that record engineer access are retained for at least 12 months (our control requires 12 months of audit trail).

If you can get me the one-line attestation and confirmation on audit log retention by EOD Monday (May 24) that would keep us on schedule.

Thanks,
Brad

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwood.com>
Cc: Brad Keller <brad.keller@medisecure.com>
Date: Sat, 22 May 2026 11:08:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: SIG follow-up: KV cache + training question — attestation & audit log retention

Marcus/Vivek — forwarding Brad's asks (procurement at MediSecure). He needs: 1) a single-line attestation we can sign and 2) confirmation that our audit access logs are retained >= 12 months. Draft language below that I used in the PDF; please review and let me know if Legal is comfortable with the attestation and whether Compliance can confirm the log retention policy.

Draft attestation (suggested):
"Redwood Inference attests that customer prompts, responses, and inference state are not used to train or fine-tune model weights without explicit, written customer consent. Access to customer data is logged and restricted to authorized personnel; audit logs are retained in accordance with Redwood's SOC 2 controls."

Questions: 
- Can we add a parenthetical to explicitly say audit logs are retained for 12 months? (procurement needs that).
- Is the attestation ok to be signed by our Head of Legal / Compliance, or does it need executive sign-off?

Thanks — Aisha

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Brad Keller <brad.keller@medisecure.com>
Date: Sat, 22 May 2026 16:40:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SIG follow-up: KV cache + training question

Hi Brad — quick update and final artifacts attached. Per Legal/Compliance review:  

- Signed attestation (one-line):
"Redwood Inference attests that customer prompts, responses, and inference state are not used to train or fine-tune model weights without explicit, written customer consent. Access to customer data is logged and restricted to authorized personnel; audit logs are retained for a minimum of 12 months in accordance with Redwood's SOC 2 controls."

- Audit log retention: Compliance confirmed audit logs (access and admin actions relevant to customer data) are retained for at least 12 months by default for hosted and Dedicated control planes. Private/on-prem customers can configure retention in contract.

Attached: 1) redwood-sig-answers-20260522.pdf (procurement-ready statement + signed attestation) 2) soc2-controls-summary-2026.pdf (control mappings and retention table).

If you want the attestation on company letterhead with an actual signature image, I can get Legal to provide that and send over a dated PDF — say let me know if that is required for your procurement packet.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

title: SIG: KV-cache retention & model training commitments
preview:
From: Brad Keller <brad.keller@medisecure.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: MediSecure Procurement <procurement@medisecure.com>
Date: Thu, 20 May 2026 10:12:00 -0700
Subject: SIG follow-up: KV cache + training question

Hi Aisha,

Thanks for the SIG answers you provided earlier — our security team has one last set of clarifying questions before we close the questionnaire: specifically about the inference KV cache and retention/usage of request-response logs for model training. Could you confirm:  

- Whether the KV cache (model key/value state used during inference) is ever written to durable storage or logs, and if so what the retention window is.
- The retention window for request/response logs (prompts and model outputs) for your hosted API by default, and whether that can be configured to 0 for managed enterprise deployments.
- Whether customer prompt/response content is ever used to further train or fine-tune models without an explicit opt-in.

We need a short, procurement-ready statement we can attach to our SIG response (one paragraph + any relevant artifacts). Also let us know whether you can provide an exported evidence PDF or SOC 2 control mapping for our records.

Appreciate the quick help — we are on a tight procurement timeline.

Thanks,
Brad
Brad Keller
Senior InfoSec Analyst, MediSecure Health
security@medisecure.com



From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Brad Keller <brad.keller@medisecure.com>
Date: Fri, 21 May 2026 09:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SIG follow-up: KV cache + training question

Hi Brad — thanks for the clear list. Below is a concise, procurement-friendly statement followed by the technical details. I attached a short PDF with the same wording plus a SOC 2 controls summary.

Procurement statement (one-paragraph):
Redwood Inference treats customer request/response content and inference state as customer data. By default, hosted API request/response logs are retained for 30 days to support debugging, abuse detection, and service reliability; customers on Redwood Dedicated or Private deployments may configure log retention to meet contractual requirements, including zero retention. The KV cache used during inference is an in-memory/session state and is not persisted to long-term storage as part of normal operation. Redwood does not use customer prompt or response content to train or fine-tune models without explicit, opt-in written consent. Audit access to customer data is tightly controlled, logged, and governed by our SOC 2 controls.

Technical details / clarifications:
- KV cache:  
  - The KV cache (attention keys/values and ephemeral decoding state) exists in RAM on the serving node for the duration of the session. It may be serialized in memory for intra-cluster transfer during scaling operations but is not written to durable customer-accessible object stores as part of normal inference. Redwood engineers do not persist KV cache to long-term storage except under rare, documented incident response workflows (and those actions are logged and subject to approval).
- Prompt/response logs (hosted):  
  - Default retention: 30 days. Purpose: debugging, abuse detection, quality monitoring.
  - Enterprise/Dedicated/Private: retention is configurable in contract; default for Dedicated can be set to match customer requirements including 0 days (no retention).
- Training on customer data:  
  - Redwood does not use customer prompt/response content to train or update model weights unless the customer provides explicit opt-in via a written agreement. Aggregated telemetry and non-identifiable operational metrics may be used to improve platform reliability but are not traceable to individual prompt content.
- Access controls and logging:  
  - Access to raw request/response data is strictly limited to authorized roles (SRE and Support under break-glass with approvals). All such access is logged and reviewed as part of SOC 2 change and access control processes.

If you need a short signed attestation line for procurement ("Redwood attests that..."), let me know and I can coordinate with Legal to provide a dated statement.

Attached: redwood-sig-answers-20260522.pdf (procurement statement + control mapping), soc2-controls-summary-2026.pdf

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
support@redwood.com



From: Brad Keller <brad.keller@medisecure.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 21 May 2026 14:15:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SIG follow-up: KV cache + training question

Aisha — this is great, thank you. Two quick asks before we mark the SIG row as closed:  

1) Procurement wants a single-line attestation we can drop into our vendor register (e.g., "Redwood attests that customer data is not used to train models without explicit consent"). Can Legal sign/date that?
2) Can you confirm whether audit logs that record engineer access are retained for at least 12 months (our control requires 12 months of audit trail).

If you can get me the one-line attestation and confirmation on audit log retention by EOD Monday (May 24) that would keep us on schedule.

Thanks,
Brad



From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwood.com>
Cc: Brad Keller <brad.keller@medisecure.com>
Date: Sat, 22 May 2026 11:08:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: SIG follow-up: KV cache + training question — attestation & audit log retention

Marcus/Vivek — forwarding Brad's asks (procurement at MediSecure). He needs: 1) a single-line attestation we can sign and 2) confirmation that our audit access logs are retained >= 12 months. Draft language below that I used in the PDF; please review and let me know if Legal is comfortable with the attestation and whether Compliance can confirm the log retention policy.

Draft attestation (suggested):
"Redwood Inference attests that customer prompts, responses, and inference state are not used to train or fine-tune model weights without explicit, written customer consent. Access to customer data is logged and restricted to authorized personnel; audit logs are retained in accordance with Redwood's SOC 2 controls."

Questions: 
- Can we add a parenthetical to explicitly say audit logs are retained for 12 months? (procurement needs that).
- Is the attestation ok to be signed by our Head of Legal / Compliance, or does it need executive sign-off?

Thanks — Aisha



From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Brad Keller <brad.keller@medisecure.com>
Date: Sat, 22 May 2026 16:40:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SIG follow-up: KV cache + training question

Hi Brad — quick update and final artifacts attached. Per Legal/Compliance review:  

- Signed attestation (one-line):
"Redwood Inference attests that customer prompts, responses, and inference state are not used to train or fine-tune model weights without explicit, written customer consent. Access to customer data is logged and restricted to authorized personnel; audit logs are retained for a minimum of 12 months in accordance with Redwood's SOC 2 controls."

- Audit log retention: Compliance confirmed audit logs (access and admin actions relevant to customer data) are retained for at least 12 months by default for hosted and Dedicated control planes. Private/on-prem customers can configure retention in contract.

Attached: 1) redwood-sig-answers-20260522.pdf (procurement-ready statement + signed attestation) 2) soc2-controls-summary-2026.pdf (control mappings and retention table).

If you want the attestation on company letterhead with an actual signature image, I can get Legal to provide that and send over a dated PDF — say let me know if that is required for your procurement packet.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
support@redwood.com

Attachments: redwood-sig-answers-20260522.pdf (procurement statement + attestation), soc2-controls-summary-2026.pdf

source.subject: Reserved capacity tenor & GPU cost contingency — Velociti AI
messages:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Sam Rios <sam@velociti.ai>, procurement@velociti.ai
Date: 2026-05-18T09:12:00Z
Subject: Reserved capacity tenor & GPU cost contingency — Velociti AI

Team / Sam,

Following our demo and sizing call last week, Velociti is verbally committing to a reserved GPU pool, but procurement asked for clarity on how we handle GPU market price swings in the contract. They want a disciplined approach to discounts/credits tied to market cost changes. I attached a draft SOW and a simple scenario workbook showing a few market moves.

Key asks for internal alignment:
- Recommended contract tenor (12 vs 24 months) and the commercial tradeoffs
- A simple contingency mechanism (e.g., tiered credits or monthly true-up) we can offer without exposing Redwood to open-ended downside
- Approval thresholds for discounts (who signs off at what % off list)

Sam / procurement: we want to give you a clear, implementable schedule so procurement can route approvals on their side. If helpful, we can propose a canary clause for the first 3 months.

Attachments: VEL-2317_Draft_SOW.pdf, GPU_Price_Scenarios.xlsx

Next steps: internal recommendations by EOD so I can send a proposal to Sam before EOD tomorrow.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Head of Sales, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0192
From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: Sam Rios <sam@velociti.ai>, procurement@velociti.ai
Date: 2026-05-18T10:03:00Z
Subject: Re: Reserved capacity tenor & GPU cost contingency — Velociti AI

Ava / team — quick thoughts:

1) Tenor recommendation: aim for a 12-month firm term with an optional 12-month extension at pre-agreed rates. That lowers our tail risk and gives Velociti runway.

2) Contingency mechanism (simple, cap-limited):
   - Define baseline GPU cost index (monthly average of public spot/wholesale price)
   - If index rises >20% vs baseline for two consecutive months -> customer receives a service credit equal to X% of monthly committed spend (propose 10%) capped at 2 months per 12-mo period
   - If index falls >20% -> we consider downward pass-through at pre-agreed tiers (helps procurement)

3) Approval thresholds: discounts/credits <=10% can be approved by Sales Director (Ava), >10% up to 20% requires Finance (Marcus) sign-off, >20% needs VP/exec approval.

I attached a template credit schedule we use with smaller customers — we can adapt. If Marcus can share the downside P&L hit at 10%/15%/20% that will help finalize thresholds.

-Karthik
From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: Sam Rios <sam@velociti.ai>, procurement@velociti.ai
Date: 2026-05-18T12:27:00Z
Subject: Re: Reserved capacity tenor & GPU cost contingency — Velociti AI

Ava / Karthik — did a quick sensitivity using the attached commit_model_v3.csv (summarized below). Numbers are illustrative but directionally accurate:

- Baseline: committed monthly spend = $180k (Velociti sizing)
- 10% market price increase -> incremental cost ~ $18k/month (we'd absorb vs credit if not capped)
- 10% credit to customer equals $18k; at 3 months that is $54k (material)

Recommendation:
- Keep credit cap per 12-mo window (e.g., 2 months max) and limit single-event exposure (prevents blowups if market spikes).
- Use a tiered approach (10% credit for 20–30% price move; 15% credit for 30–50% move; >50% -> automatic review + exec approval).

Operational note: we need a clear index provider and a monthly reconciliation process (finance + billing). Without that we can't reconcile credits reliably. I can own the reconciliation design but need product/ops commitment.

Attaching commit_model_v3.csv for the numbers. Approve routing rules and I'll break out P&L scenarios for legal/contract.

-Marcus
From: Sam Rios <sam@velociti.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: procurement@velociti.ai
Date: 2026-05-18T13:05:00Z
Subject: Re: Reserved capacity tenor & GPU cost contingency — Velociti AI

Ava / all,

Thanks for the quick turn. Procurement needs a deterministic schedule they can include in their purchase order. Our preference is:

- 12-month firm term + 12-month option
- A clear discount/credit table tied to a public GPU cost index (we use vendorSpotIndex.com)
- Cap and trigger definitions so our CFO can sign off

If you can send a redlineable table and a one-paragraph explanation of the reconciliation process, procurement can route legal and finance in parallel. We can also sign a time-limited canary for the first quarter if Redwood wants to limit long-term exposure.

Sam Rios
Head of Infrastructure, Velociti AI
sam@velociti.ai
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Sam Rios <sam@velociti.ai>, procurement@velociti.ai
Date: 2026-05-18T15:02:00Z
Subject: Re: Reserved capacity tenor & GPU cost contingency — Velociti AI

Thanks everyone — good alignment. Action items I will take this afternoon:

1) Draft a one-page contingency table (tiered triggers, caps, index source) and attach as an exhibit to the SOW.
2) Propose approval flow: Ava (Sales) up to 10%; Marcus (Finance) 10–20%; Laura (Legal) looped for >15% language; VP Sales for anything >20%.
3) Share the redlineable SOW + exhibit with Sam and procurement by 09:00 PT tomorrow.

Marcus: please send the P&L scenarios by EOD so I can include the recommended caps. Karthik: please send the small-customer template if you still have it.

Will mark this as high-priority — thank you.

Regards,
Ava Chen
Head of Sales, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0192

Sam / procurement: we want to give you a clear, implementable schedule so procurement can route approvals on their side. If helpful, we can propose a canary clause for the first 3 months.

Attachments: VEL-2317_Draft_SOW.pdf, GPU_Price_Scenarios.xlsx

Next steps: internal recommendations by EOD so I can send a proposal to Sam before EOD tomorrow.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Head of Sales, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0192


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: Sam Rios <sam@velociti.ai>, procurement@velociti.ai
Date: 2026-05-18T10:03:00Z
Subject: Re: Reserved capacity tenor & GPU cost contingency — Velociti AI

Ava / team — quick thoughts:

1) Tenor recommendation: aim for a 12-month firm term with an optional 12-month extension at pre-agreed rates. That lowers our tail risk and gives Velociti runway.

2) Contingency mechanism (simple, cap-limited):
   - Define baseline GPU cost index (monthly average of public spot/wholesale price)
   - If index rises >20% vs baseline for two consecutive months -> customer receives a service credit equal to X% of monthly committed spend (propose 10%) capped at 2 months per 12-mo period
   - If index falls >20% -> we consider downward pass-through at pre-agreed tiers (helps procurement)

3) Approval thresholds: discounts/credits <=10% can be approved by Sales Director (Ava), >10% up to 20% requires Finance (Marcus) sign-off, >20% needs VP/exec approval.

I attached a template credit schedule we use with smaller customers — we can adapt. If Marcus can share the downside P&L hit at 10%/15%/20% that will help finalize thresholds.

-Karthik


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: Sam Rios <sam@velociti.ai>, procurement@velociti.ai
Date: 2026-05-18T12:27:00Z
Subject: Re: Reserved capacity tenor & GPU cost contingency — Velociti AI

Ava / Karthik — did a quick sensitivity using the attached commit_model_v3.csv (summarized below). Numbers are illustrative but directionally accurate:

- Baseline: committed monthly spend = $180k (Velociti sizing)
- 10% market price increase -> incremental cost ~ $18k/month (we'd absorb vs credit if not capped)
- 10% credit to customer equals $18k; at 3 months that is $54k (material)

Recommendation:
- Keep credit cap per 12-mo window (e.g., 2 months max) and limit single-event exposure (prevents blowups if market spikes).
- Use a tiered approach (10% credit for 20–30% price move; 15% credit for 30–50% move; >50% -> automatic review + exec approval).

Operational note: we need a clear index provider and a monthly reconciliation process (finance + billing). Without that we can't reconcile credits reliably. I can own the reconciliation design but need product/ops commitment.

Attaching commit_model_v3.csv for the numbers. Approve routing rules and I'll break out P&L scenarios for legal/contract.

-Marcus


From: Sam Rios <sam@velociti.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: procurement@velociti.ai
Date: 2026-05-18T13:05:00Z
Subject: Re: Reserved capacity tenor & GPU cost contingency — Velociti AI

Ava / all,

Thanks for the quick turn. Procurement needs a deterministic schedule they can include in their purchase order. Our preference is:

- 12-month firm term + 12-month option
- A clear discount/credit table tied to a public GPU cost index (we use vendorSpotIndex.com)
- Cap and trigger definitions so our CFO can sign off

If you can send a redlineable table and a one-paragraph explanation of the reconciliation process, procurement can route legal and finance in parallel. We can also sign a time-limited canary for the first quarter if Redwood wants to limit long-term exposure.

Sam Rios
Head of Infrastructure, Velociti AI
sam@velociti.ai


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Sam Rios <sam@velociti.ai>, procurement@velociti.ai
Date: 2026-05-18T15:02:00Z
Subject: Re: Reserved capacity tenor & GPU cost contingency — Velociti AI

Thanks everyone — good alignment. Action items I will take this afternoon:

1) Draft a one-page contingency table (tiered triggers, caps, index source) and attach as an exhibit to the SOW.
2) Propose approval flow: Ava (Sales) up to 10%; Marcus (Finance) 10–20%; Laura (Legal) looped for >15% language; VP Sales for anything >20%.
3) Share the redlineable SOW + exhibit with Sam and procurement by 09:00 PT tomorrow.

Marcus: please send the P&L scenarios by EOD so I can include the recommended caps. Karthik: please send the small-customer template if you still have it.

Will mark this as high-priority — thank you.

Regards,
Ava Chen
Head of Sales, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0192

source.subject: MAP: Ramp & Pacing Playbook for Final Performance Eval
messages:
From: Emma Price <emma@sage-analytics.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: platform@sage-analytics.com
Date: 2026-05-05T09:15:00-07:00
Subject: MAP: final perf eval — clarifying pacing expectations

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the walkthrough yesterday. As we finalize the MAP for the POC close, the platform team asked for a short playbook that defines the ramp/pacing profiles we'll run and the pass/fail gating for TTFT, sustained throughput, and queue length under load. They want to understand how Redwood will validate burst vs steady-state behavior and any recommended rate-limit tuning to avoid head-of-line blocking.

Can you share a proposed schedule (dates/times), the exact ramp scripts you plan to run, and the acceptance thresholds your team will use? We need this to commit the final sign-off on 5/12.

TIA — Emma

Emma Price
Director, Platform Engineering | Sage Analytics
[Attachment: spec-snapshot.pdf (PDF)]

From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Emma Price <emma@sage-analytics.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-05-05T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: final perf eval — clarifying pacing expectations

Emma,

Appreciate the clear ask — attaching a compact playbook and ramp script. Summary here so it's in the thread:

Planned window: 2026-05-06 09:00–13:00 PT (warmup + 3 profiles) and 2026-05-07 10:00–14:00 PT (regression and final acceptance).
Profiles:
- Spike profile (sawtooth bursts): 30s ramp to 8x baseline QPS, hold 60s, drop to baseline; repeated 12x over 40 minutes to stress concurrency and observe queue shedding.
- Ramp-and-hold (steady-state): linear ramp from baseline to target over 5 minutes, hold for 30 minutes at each target (targets = 1x, 2x, 4x, 6x baseline throughput).
- Micro-burst series: 5 x 10s microbursts at 12x baseline to observe head-of-line blocking and KV-cache thrash.

Key metrics we will capture (token-level where possible):
- TTFT (time-to-first-token) median, p95, p99 by model & route
- Token/sec throughput (observed) and accepted QPS (ingress)
- Service queue depth and per-worker pending requests
- Error rate and 429s; origin of 429 (ingress vs internal queue drop)
- Cold vs warmed KV-cache behavior via prefix-hit tagging

Acceptance gating (pass/fail):
- TTFT: p95 <= 200ms, p99 <= 500ms for steady-state at the committed target; allowance for transient spikes during 30s bursts but p99 <= 800ms for bursts.
- Sustained throughput: observed token/sec within +/-15% of target for steady-hold windows (30m) without sustained queue growth.
- Queueing: service queue depth must stabilize after warmup; no sustained increase > 50% baseline for more than 5 minutes.

Rate-limit recommendations: windowed throttling at ingress per-customer with a short token-bucket to smooth microbursts; add an adaptive lane that allows brief overcommit for warmup but drops large bursts that cause queue blowups. We'll include exact bucket settings in ramp_script.yaml.

Attaching: ramp_script.yaml, pacing_profile_definitions.md, queueing_observability-checklist.pdf.

If the gate thresholds look OK, we'll lock the schedule and run a dry-run the morning of 5/6.

Thanks,
Ava

Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
[Attachment: ramp_script.yaml (YAML)]

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Emma Price <emma@sage-analytics.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-05-05T12:10:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: final perf eval — clarifying pacing expectations

Quick technical notes from runtime side:

1) For burst profiles we should enable per-worker request queue caps and early drop with a distinct 429 code so we can separate ingress limiter hits from internal pressure drops. We'll tag dropped requests for traceability.
2) Set KV-cache warming: pre-seed prefixes for the longest prompt sets to reduce cold-TTFT variance. I'll attach a short snippet for the loader.
3) Observability: ensure token-level timings are exported to the tracing backend and we capture per-model kernel-switch counts — that'll help debug outliers.

Also: during the first spike run let's keep autoscaler off for deterministic results; we'll re-enable on 5/07 for end-to-end check.

-Karthik

[Attachment: kv_warm_loader.py (PY)]

From: Emma Price <emma@sage-analytics.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Cc: platform@sage-analytics.com
Date: 2026-05-06T08:02:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: final perf eval — clarifying pacing expectations

Thanks — schedule looks good. Two quick asks before the dry-run:
1) Can you add a short column in the results CSV that maps each sample to the MAP milestone (e.g., "warmup", "spike-1", "hold-4").
2) For the rate-limit tuning: can you propose two sets of token-bucket params (conservative and aggressive) so we can discuss tradeoffs with security/procurement?

We'll be ready for the dry-run at 09:00 PT.

—Emma

From: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
To: Emma Price <emma@sage-analytics.com>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-05-06T10:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: final perf eval — clarifying pacing expectations

Emma/Team — following the dry-run we've uploaded preliminary results to the shared drive and a compact CSV (pacing-results-20260507.csv). Observations:

- Dry-run spike series produced transient p99 TTFT ~740ms with a single 8-minute window where queue depth trended up; root cause was ingress burst smoothing not applied on their generator.
- Steady-state holds (2x and 4x) met the steady gating: p95 TTFT 120–180ms, p99 320–420ms, observed token/sec within +/-10% of target.
- Micro-bursts exposed a head-of-line effect for 1% of requests tied to an older kernel path (we flagged and pinned those workers in the run).

Actions:
- We'll apply the conservative token-bucket (params in the attached) and re-run the spike suite tomorrow 10:00 PT.
- We'll annotate the CSV with MAP milestone labels per Emma's request.

Next: confirm 05/07 10:00 for the acceptance run where we'll enable autoscaler and validate fallbacks.

-Marcus

[Attachment: pacing-results-20260507.csv (CSV)]

From: Emma Price <emma@sage-analytics.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Cc: platform@sage-analytics.com
Date: 2026-05-07T16:42:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: Ramp & Pacing Playbook for Final Performance Eval — acceptance confirmation

Team — thanks for the quick turnaround. We've reviewed the annotated CSV and the conservative rate-limit run. The acceptance criteria are satisfied for the steady-state windows and the mitigations for the micro-burst head-of-line issue look reasonable. Sage will sign the MAP contingent on the following:

1) Redwood to provide final ramp_script.yaml and the two token-bucket param sets in a single doc.
2) A post-run summary with the final per-route p95/p99 TTFT and a short note on which workers were pinned and why.
3) A confirmation that autoscaler behavior (enabled in the 05/07 run) did not invalidate the steady-state gating.

If you can provide those by EOD 05/10 we'll finish procurement paperwork and target a production enablement on 06/01.

Emma

Emma Price
Director, Platform Engineering | Sage Analytics
[Attachment: procurement-req.docx (DOCX)]

1) Redwood to provide final ramp_script.yaml and the two token-bucket param sets in a single doc.
2) A post-run summary with the final per-route p95/p99 TTFT and a short note on which workers were pinned and why.
3) A confirmation that autoscaler behavior (enabled in the 05/07 run) did not invalidate the steady-state gating.

If you can provide those by EOD 05/10 we'll finish procurement paperwork and target a production enablement on 06/01.

Emma

Emma Price
Director, Platform Engineering | Sage Analytics
[Attachment: procurement-req.docx (DOCX)]

source.subject: Spike in 5xx/429s — SDK retry amplification (FinVanta)
messages:
From: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finvanta.com>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 2 May 2026 09:10:00 -0700
Subject: Elevated 5xx/429s and timeouts impacting transactions

Hi Evelyn,

We're seeing a high volume of 5xx and 429 responses against Redwood starting ~08:45 PDT this morning which is causing timeouts in our payment processing pipeline and a lot of retries on our side. Latency across affected requests has jumped from ~120ms to >2s depending on burst. Our monitoring shows our client SDK attempted exponential retries and appears to be amplifying load during the spike.

Attached: finvanta_request-traces_20260502.json — it's a small sample of traces and request IDs.

This is impacting customer transactions and the ops team is escalating. Can you advise immediate mitigations we can apply (client/server) while you investigate? We need a quick plan to stop the retry amplification.

Thanks,
Samantha Lee
Platform Reliability Lead
FinVanta

--
FinVanta platform-ops: platform-ops@finvanta.com
From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finvanta.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 2 May 2026 09:18:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Elevated 5xx/429s and timeouts impacting transactions

Samantha — thanks for the quick heads-up and for the traces. We're escalating this to our incident response and engineering immediately.

Immediate actions we are taking (next 30–90m):
- Triage: Nikhil is pulling server-side rates and token-bucket metrics and will look for retry amplification patterns.
- Temporary mitigation: we'll propose a short-lived server-side per-account throttling and a 10s circuit-breaker for rapid recurrence if amplification is detected.
- Ask for your help: can you temporarily reduce client-side retries to at most 2 attempts and add jitter (200–800ms) while we validate? That change should reduce amplification fast.

I'll follow up with a concrete runbook and a timeline. Appreciate the attachments — we'll correlate.

Evelyn Hart
Head of Customer Success, Redwood Inference
From: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwood.ai>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Cc: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finvanta.com>
Date: Sat, 2 May 2026 09:36:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Elevated 5xx/429s and timeouts impacting transactions

On Sat, May 2, 2026 at 09:18, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Samantha — thanks for the quick heads-up...

Quick analysis from the initial logs I pulled:
- We see a spike in 429s followed by retries from the same client prefixes. Many clients are configured with aggressive default retry policies (exp backoff but very short initial window and no jitter).
- The request distribution shows repeated bursts within <1s windows (retry loops) for the same user sessions — classic retry amplification.
- Server-side, the per-account token bucket for FinVanta exhausted and we started returning 429s; this triggered more retries which produced transient 5xx errors downstream when queues filled.

Recommended immediate technical mitigations (we can implement within 45m):
1) Apply a temporary per-account throttling window and a 10s cooldown if a client exceeds 50% of its steady-state request rate. This will reduce the amplification loop.
2) Enable a short-lived circuit breaker: after N 429s in M seconds, return an informative 429 with Retry-After header of 10s and a structured JSON recommending client backoff.
3) On our SDK side: add a safety cap that prevents >3 retries by default and enables randomized jitter. We can push a conservative hotfix flag for SDKs used by FinVanta (if they're using our SDK).

I'll attach the rate-limiter CSV and a short runbook excerpt.

-Nikhil
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachments: rate-limiter-metrics-0502.csv
From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finvanta.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 2 May 2026 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Temporary client-side recommendations and quick patch

Hi Samantha — thanks for sharing the traces. Two immediate, low-risk changes you can apply on your side within minutes:

1) Client SDK config (recommended): set max_attempts=2, base_backoff=250ms, max_backoff=1200ms, enable_jitter=true (range 200–800ms).
2) If you control a small percentage of traffic, enable canary client toggles so the change targets 10% first.

If you confirm the SDK version you are using (send package.json or SDK version string), we can tell you whether the SDK has a built-in safety cap you can flip on.

We will:
- Stage a server-side throttling rule and the circuit breaker within 45m.
- Open a post-incident runbook and propose a follow-up for a longer-term fix (SDK defaults + server-side defense).

Attached: redwood_mitigation_runbook_v1.pdf — contains exact curl examples and a short validation checklist.

Monica Patel
Customer Success Engineer, Redwood Inference
From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 2 May 2026 11:17:00 -07:00
Subject: Exec update (FYI) — FinVanta incident and ask for credit approval

Evelyn/Rachel — brief update for partner leadership:
- Impact: FinVanta production saw ~18% failed transactions during the 08:45–10:30 window, customer reported revenue-impacting timeouts.
- Root cause (initial): retry amplification due to aggressive client-side defaults + temporary exhaustion of per-account rate buckets.
- Immediate fix: coordinated client-side throttling + server-side cooldown/circuit-breaker rolled out by eng.
- Ask: customer is requesting a small one-time service credit while we complete the postmortem and deliver an SDK safety update. Can leadership approve up to a $7,500 credit (commercial team to document)?

I'll hold on external comms until we have the postmortem draft and a remediation timeline.

-Marcus Lin
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
From: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finvanta.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Sun, 3 May 2026 15:47:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Spike in 5xx/429s — SDK retry amplification (FinVanta)

title: Spike in 5xx/429s — SDK retry amplification (FinVanta)
preview:
From: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finvanta.com>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 2 May 2026 09:10:00 -0700
Subject: Elevated 5xx/429s and timeouts impacting transactions

Hi Evelyn,

We're seeing a high volume of 5xx and 429 responses against Redwood starting ~08:45 PDT this morning which is causing timeouts in our payment processing pipeline and a lot of retries on our side. Latency across affected requests has jumped from ~120ms to >2s depending on burst. Our monitoring shows our client SDK attempted exponential retries and appears to be amplifying load during the spike.

Attached: finvanta_request-traces_20260502.json — it's a small sample of traces and request IDs.

This is impacting customer transactions and the ops team is escalating. Can you advise immediate mitigations we can apply (client/server) while you investigate? We need a quick plan to stop the retry amplification.

Thanks,
Samantha Lee
Platform Reliability Lead
FinVanta

--
FinVanta platform-ops: platform-ops@finvanta.com


From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finvanta.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 2 May 2026 09:18:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Elevated 5xx/429s and timeouts impacting transactions

Samantha — thanks for the quick heads-up and for the traces. We're escalating this to our incident response and engineering immediately.

Immediate actions we are taking (next 30–90m):
- Triage: Nikhil is pulling server-side rates and token-bucket metrics and will look for retry amplification patterns.
- Temporary mitigation: we'll propose a short-lived server-side per-account throttling and a 10s circuit-breaker for rapid recurrence if amplification is detected.
- Ask for your help: can you temporarily reduce client-side retries to at most 2 attempts and add jitter (200–800ms) while we validate? That change should reduce amplification fast.

I'll follow up with a concrete runbook and a timeline. Appreciate the attachments — we'll correlate.

Evelyn Hart
Head of Customer Success, Redwood Inference


From: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwood.ai>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Cc: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finvanta.com>
Date: Sat, 2 May 2026 09:36:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Elevated 5xx/429s and timeouts impacting transactions

On Sat, May 2, 2026 at 09:18, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Samantha — thanks for the quick heads-up...

Quick analysis from the initial logs I pulled:
- We see a spike in 429s followed by retries from the same client prefixes. Many clients are configured with aggressive default retry policies (exp backoff but very short initial window and no jitter).
- The request distribution shows repeated bursts within <1s windows (retry loops) for the same user sessions — classic retry amplification.
- Server-side, the per-account token bucket for FinVanta exhausted and we started returning 429s; this triggered more retries which produced transient 5xx errors downstream when queues filled.

Recommended immediate technical mitigations (we can implement within 45m):
1) Apply a temporary per-account throttling window and a 10s cooldown if a client exceeds 50% of its steady-state request rate. This will reduce the amplification loop.
2) Enable a short-lived circuit breaker: after N 429s in M seconds, return an informative 429 with Retry-After header of 10s and a structured JSON recommending client backoff.
3) On our SDK side: add a safety cap that prevents >3 retries by default and enables randomized jitter. We can push a conservative hotfix flag for SDKs used by FinVanta (if they're using our SDK).

I'll attach the rate-limiter CSV and a short runbook excerpt.

-Nikhil
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachments: rate-limiter-metrics-0502.csv


From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finvanta.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 2 May 2026 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Temporary client-side recommendations and quick patch

Hi Samantha — thanks for sharing the traces. Two immediate, low-risk changes you can apply on your side within minutes:

1) Client SDK config (recommended): set max_attempts=2, base_backoff=250ms, max_backoff=1200ms, enable_jitter=true (range 200–800ms).
2) If you control a small percentage of traffic, enable canary client toggles so the change targets 10% first.

If you confirm the SDK version you are using (send package.json or SDK version string), we can tell you whether the SDK has a built-in safety cap you can flip on.

We will:
- Stage a server-side throttling rule and the circuit breaker within 45m.
- Open a post-incident runbook and propose a follow-up for a longer-term fix (SDK defaults + server-side defense).

Attached: redwood_mitigation_runbook_v1.pdf — contains exact curl examples and a short validation checklist.

Monica Patel
Customer Success Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 2 May 2026 11:17:00 -07:00
Subject: Exec update (FYI) — FinVanta incident and ask for credit approval

Evelyn/Rachel — brief update for partner leadership:
- Impact: FinVanta production saw ~18% failed transactions during the 08:45–10:30 window, customer reported revenue-impacting timeouts.
- Root cause (initial): retry amplification due to aggressive client-side defaults + temporary exhaustion of per-account rate buckets.
- Immediate fix: coordinated client-side throttling + server-side cooldown/circuit-breaker rolled out by eng.
- Ask: customer is requesting a small one-time service credit while we complete the postmortem and deliver an SDK safety update. Can leadership approve up to a $7,500 credit (commercial team to document)?

I'll hold on external comms until we have the postmortem draft and a remediation timeline.

-Marcus Lin
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

source.subject: Model shortlist + token buckets for LedgerPay onboarding
messages:
From: Ellis Turner <ellis@ledgerpay.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>
Cc: platform-eng@ledgerpay.com
Date: Mon, 27 Apr 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick follow on model fit inputs

Hi Ben / Monica,

Thanks for the demo last week — team is excited. I pulled together a quick token profile from our staging logs and a couple of operational constraints:

- Primary flows: invoice auto-reconciliation (short prompts, avg 120 tokens), customer dispute triage (interactive, avg 400 tokens, often includes 2–3 prior messages as context), and batch OCR post-processing (longer prompts for extraction, avg 1,800 tokens occasionally hitting 3k for multi-page).
- Estimated steady-state QPS: 2 r/s across the three flows, with 20% peak windows that could push to 6 r/s for 5–10 minutes.
- Latency targets: <220ms P50 for single-turn reconciliation, <600ms P95 for interactive triage (we can tolerate slightly higher tail for long batch jobs).
- Security: we need SOC2 evidence and confirmation about KV/prefix caching controls (we cannot cache PII).

Attaching the staging token export. Can you propose a shortlist of models and an initial token-bucket sizing approach for onboarding? We're targeting a 6-week pilot starting mid-May.

Ellis
LedgerPay — Platform

Attachment: ledgerpay_token_profile.xlsx (xlsx, 55KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Ellis Turner <ellis@ledgerpay.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 27 Apr 2026 11:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Quick follow on model fit inputs

Ellis — thanks for the export and the latency targets; super helpful. High level recommendation and clarifying questions below.

1) Shortlist (starter + spares):
   - M1-small-8q (fast 8-bit quantized variant) — candidate for reconciliation (low latency, cheap).
   - M2-compat-16 (full FP16) — fallback for interactive triage when quality matters.
   - LLM-long-32k (sized for OCR and long multi-page context) — reserve for batch OCR jobs.

2) Token-bucket sketch for pilot (per-minute units):
   - Steady-state reserve: 120k tokens/min across pool (covers 2 r/s with headroom).
   - Peak buffer (20% windows): additional 60k tokens/min allocated to a burst bucket for 10 mins.
   - Long-job pool: separate lane with pre-warmed instances for jobs >2k tokens to avoid head-of-line blocking.

Questions to confirm:
- Are the PII fields stripped before sending to Redwood, or do you need an in-flight redaction step?
- Can the batch OCR be scheduled in a 2-4 hour window if needed, or is it real-time?

I've attached a compact model shortlist PDF with perf numbers from our verify runs. Monica/Grace — can you weigh in on caching and the pilot scope? I'll follow up with a rough cost estimate once we lock the lane sizing.

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Customer Success — Redwood Inference

Attachment: model_shortlist_v2.pdf (pdf, 120KB)

From: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ellis Turner <ellis@ledgerpay.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 28 Apr 2026 09:38:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Quick follow on model fit inputs

Thanks Ben — good start. A few operational notes and a proposed pilot split: 

- Caching: we can enable prefix/KV caching for non-PII conversational context. For PII-sensitive fields, we will mark those requests "no-cache" at the route level. That satisfies your SOC2 control requirement (we'll provide the audit config).
- Autoscaling policy: suggest a two-lane policy — low-latency lane (M1-small-8q) with continuous batching knob tightened for sub-300ms P50, and an accuracy lane (M2-compat-16) that uses slightly looser batching to meet quality. Long batch OCR jobs should route to the LLM-long-32k pool which can be pre-warmed at scheduled intervals to reduce cold-starts.
- Observability: we'll add a temporary trace tag for the pilot routes and surface token-level latency/cost in the Console dashboard.

Pilot proposal (6 weeks):
Week 1: Integration & baseline runs — run 48-hour smoke, confirm PII flags, validate end-to-end latency.
Weeks 2–4: Controlled ramp — shift 20/40/60% of production traffic to Redwood lanes, measure quality regressions with the supplied eval set.
Weeks 5–6: Peak soak + runbook handover — exercise 20% peak windows and finalize docs for handover to your SRE.

Operational ask from LedgerPay: please confirm whether OCR batches can be deferred to a 2-hour window for pre-warming optimization; if not, we'll propose a higher sustained allocation for the long-job pool.

Marcus — can you prepare a quick cost delta for tightening batching vs. moving traffic to M2? I'll attach a draft pilot scope doc (pilot_scope.docx) to the HubSpot ticket.

Best,
Monica Patel
Customer Success Manager — Redwood Inference

Quoted text below:
> On Mon, 27 Apr 2026 at 11:04, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai> wrote:
> > Ellis — thanks for the export and the latency targets; super helpful.
> > 1) Shortlist (starter + spares):
> >    - M1-small-8q (fast 8-bit quantized variant) — candidate for reconciliation (low latency, cheap).
> >    - M2-compat-16 (full FP16) — fallback for interactive triage when quality matters.
> >    - LLM-long-32k (sized for OCR and long multi-page context) — reserve for batch OCR jobs.

Attachment: pilot_scope.docx (docx, 18KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Ellis Turner <ellis@ledgerpay.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 29 Apr 2026 15:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Quick follow on model fit inputs

Ellis — great, thanks for confirming earlier questions over Slack. Next steps and owners below so we can lock the pilot start date (May 18):

- LedgerPay: confirm whether OCR batches can be scheduled into a 2-hour pre-warm window (Ellis) — due by Fri 2 May.
- Redwood CS (Monica/Ben): finalize route-level no-cache rules and provide SOC2 evidence snapshot — due Tue 5 May.
- Redwood Infra (Marcus): cost delta for tightened batching vs. moving 30% traffic to M2-compat-16, and a runbook stub for long-job pool warmers — due Wed 6 May.
- Both: sign-off on pilot_scope.docx in HubSpot (deal LDGR-23).

I'll prepare the initial capacity reservation once OCR cadence is confirmed, and we'll aim to pre-warm two LLM-long-32k instances for the first 24 hours of the pilot.

One-line ack appreciated and we'll queue the kickoff calendar invite.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Customer Success — Redwood Inference

> On Tue, 28 Apr 2026 at 09:38, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai> wrote:
> > Thanks Ben — good start. A few operational notes and a proposed pilot split:
> > - Caching: we can enable prefix/KV caching for non-PII conversational context. For PII-sensitive fields, we will mark those requests "no-cache" at the route level.
> > - Autoscaling policy: suggest a two-lane policy — low-latency lane (M1-small-8q) with continuous batching knob tightened for sub-300ms P50, and an accuracy lane (M2-compat-16) that uses slightly looser batching to meet quality.

Attachment: ledgerpay_token_profile.xlsx (xlsx, 55KB)

Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 29 Apr 2026 15:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Quick follow on model fit inputs

Ellis — great, thanks for confirming earlier questions over Slack. Next steps and owners below so we can lock the pilot start date (May 18):

- LedgerPay: confirm whether OCR batches can be scheduled into a 2-hour pre-warm window (Ellis) — due by Fri 2 May.
- Redwood CS (Monica/Ben): finalize route-level no-cache rules and provide SOC2 evidence snapshot — due Tue 5 May.
- Redwood Infra (Marcus): cost delta for tightened batching vs. moving 30% traffic to M2-compat-16, and a runbook stub for long-job pool warmers — due Wed 6 May.
- Both: sign-off on pilot_scope.docx in HubSpot (deal LDGR-23).

I'll prepare the initial capacity reservation once OCR cadence is confirmed, and we'll aim to pre-warm two LLM-long-32k instances for the first 24 hours of the pilot.

One-line ack appreciated and we'll queue the kickoff calendar invite.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Customer Success — Redwood Inference

> On Tue, 28 Apr 2026 at 09:38, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai> wrote:
> > Thanks Ben — good start. A few operational notes and a proposed pilot split:
> > - Caching: we can enable prefix/KV caching for non-PII conversational context. For PII-sensitive fields, we will mark those requests "no-cache" at the route level.
> > - Autoscaling policy: suggest a two-lane policy — low-latency lane (M1-small-8q) with continuous batching knob tightened for sub-300ms P50, and an accuracy lane (M2-compat-16) that uses slightly looser batching to meet quality.

Attachment: ledgerpay_token_profile.xlsx (xlsx, 55KB)
text:
From: Ellis Turner <ellis@ledgerpay.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>
Cc: platform-eng@ledgerpay.com
Date: Mon, 27 Apr 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick follow on model fit inputs

Hi Ben / Monica,

Thanks for the demo last week — team is excited. I pulled together a quick token profile from our staging logs and a couple of operational constraints:

- Primary flows: invoice auto-reconciliation (short prompts, avg 120 tokens), customer dispute triage (interactive, avg 400 tokens, often includes 2–3 prior messages as context), and batch OCR post-processing (longer prompts for extraction, avg 1,800 tokens occasionally hitting 3k for multi-page).
- Estimated steady-state QPS: 2 r/s across the three flows, with 20% peak windows that could push to 6 r/s for 5–10 minutes.
- Latency targets: <220ms P50 for single-turn reconciliation, <600ms P95 for interactive triage (we can tolerate slightly higher tail for long batch jobs).
- Security: we need SOC2 evidence and confirmation about KV/prefix caching controls (we cannot cache PII).

Attaching the staging token export. Can you propose a shortlist of models and an initial token-bucket sizing approach for onboarding? We're targeting a 6-week pilot starting mid-May.

Ellis
LedgerPay — Platform

Attachment: ledgerpay_token_profile.xlsx (xlsx, 55KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Ellis Turner <ellis@ledgerpay.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 27 Apr 2026 11:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Quick follow on model fit inputs

Ellis — thanks for the export and the latency targets; super helpful. High level recommendation and clarifying questions below.

1) Shortlist (starter + spares):
   - M1-small-8q (fast 8-bit quantized variant) — candidate for reconciliation (low latency, cheap).
   - M2-compat-16 (full FP16) — fallback for interactive triage when quality matters.
   - LLM-long-32k (sized for OCR and long multi-page context) — reserve for batch OCR jobs.

2) Token-bucket sketch for pilot (per-minute units):
   - Steady-state reserve: 120k tokens/min across pool (covers 2 r/s with headroom).
   - Peak buffer (20% windows): additional 60k tokens/min allocated to a burst bucket for 10 mins.
   - Long-job pool: separate lane with pre-warmed instances for jobs >2k tokens to avoid head-of-line blocking.

Questions to confirm:
- Are the PII fields stripped before sending to Redwood, or do you need an in-flight redaction step?
- Can the batch OCR be scheduled in a 2-4 hour window if needed, or is it real-time?

I've attached a compact model shortlist PDF with perf numbers from our verify runs. Monica/Grace — can you weigh in on caching and the pilot scope? I'll follow up with a rough cost estimate once we lock the lane sizing.

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Customer Success — Redwood Inference

Attachment: model_shortlist_v2.pdf (pdf, 120KB)

From: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ellis Turner <ellis@ledgerpay.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 28 Apr 2026 09:38:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Quick follow on model fit inputs

Thanks Ben — good start. A few operational notes and a proposed pilot split: 

- Caching: we can enable prefix/KV caching for non-PII conversational context. For PII-sensitive fields, we will mark those requests "no-cache" at the route level. That satisfies your SOC2 control requirement (we'll provide the audit config).
- Autoscaling policy: suggest a two-lane policy — low-latency lane (M1-small-8q) with continuous batching knob tightened for sub-300ms P50, and an accuracy lane (M2-compat-16) that uses slightly looser batching to meet quality. Long batch OCR jobs should route to the LLM-long-32k pool which can be pre-warmed at scheduled intervals to reduce cold-starts.
- Observability: we'll add a temporary trace tag for the pilot routes and surface token-level latency/cost in the Console dashboard.

Pilot proposal (6 weeks):
Week 1: Integration & baseline runs — run 48-hour smoke, confirm PII flags, validate end-to-end latency.
Weeks 2–4: Controlled ramp — shift 20/40/60% of production traffic to Redwood lanes, measure quality regressions with the supplied eval set.
Weeks 5–6: Peak soak + runbook handover — exercise 20% peak windows and finalize docs for handover to your SRE.

Operational ask from LedgerPay: please confirm whether OCR batches can be deferred to a 2-hour window for pre-warming optimization; if not, we'll propose a higher sustained allocation for the long-job pool.

Marcus — can you prepare a quick cost delta for tightening batching vs. moving traffic to M2? I'll attach a draft pilot scope doc (pilot_scope.docx) to the HubSpot ticket.

Best,
Monica Patel
Customer Success Manager — Redwood Inference

> >    - M2-compat-16 (full FP16) — fallback for interactive triage when quality matters.
> >    - LLM-long-32k (sized for OCR and long multi-page context) — reserve for batch OCR jobs.

source.subject: RFI: Deployment Isolation Map, Egress Controls, and High-Level Threat Annotations
messages:
From: Jules Martin <jules.martin@wavefin.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>
Cc: Wavefin Security <security@wavefin.com>
Date: 2026-04-19T09:13:00-07:00
Subject: RFI: Deployment isolation and threat annotations - Wavefin

Hi Ben / Vanessa,

Thanks for taking this RFI. As part of our vendor security review, Wavefin needs a concise package describing how Redwood isolates tenant workloads, controls egress, and what high-level threats you analyze for production inference deployments. Specifically we are looking for: 

- A diagram showing control plane, inference zones, sidecar/proxy placement, network ACLs, and egress gateways for your Hosted and Private (VPC) deployments.
- Short annotations on plausible threat paths (e.g., cross-tenant inference data leakage, KV cache contamination, sidecar compromise) and mitigations.
- Where applicable, evidence pointers (config names, logs, or artifacts) that we can reference in our risk assessment.

We're on a tight timeline: can you provide a written response and diagrams by EOD 2026-04-20 PT? If helpful, we can schedule a 30-min call to walk the diagrams.

Thanks,
Jules
Jules Martin
Security Program Manager, Wavefin Financial

--
Attachment: Request_RFI_Wavefin_20260416.pdf (pdf stub)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Jules Martin <jules.martin@wavefin.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-04-19T11:02:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: Deployment isolation and threat annotations - Wavefin

Hi Jules — thanks for the clear ask. Looping in Vanessa, Irene and Marcus to draft a concise package. We'll aim to deliver an annotated diagram plus a short threat-summary document and supporting evidence links by tomorrow EOD PT.

Planned deliverables:
- deployment-isolation-map-v1.svg (diagram showing Hosted vs Private/VPC placement, control plane vs data plane, sidecar/proxy, egress gateways)
- threat-annotations-v1.pdf (one-page per threat with mitigations)
- security-evidence.zip (log samples, config snippets, SOC pointers)

Action items for internal team (quick):
- Vanessa: validate egress/gateway language and any customer-config constraints.
- Irene: add short evidence pointers (audit log keys and sample file names).
- Marcus: include note about KV cache boundaries and runtime hardening for inference sidecars.

Ben Carter
Senior Sales Engineer
Redwood Inference

--
Attachment: draft/deployment-isolation-map-v1.svg (diagram stub)

From: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-04-19T13:47:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: Deployment isolation and threat annotations - Wavefin

Ben / team — quick technical notes to include in the final package. Quoting Ben's planned deliverables below.

On 2026-04-19 11:02, Ben Carter wrote:
> Planned deliverables:
> - deployment-isolation-map-v1.svg
> - threat-annotations-v1.pdf
> - security-evidence.zip

Additions / clarifications (for threat-annotations and evidence index):
1) Isolation model (short bullet for diagram):
   - Control plane (Redwood-managed) lives in a distinct VPC/region with strict peering to customer Dedicated/Private nodes. Control-plane components are zero-trust: mTLS + per-service identities; no cross-tenant data stored in control plane.
   - Data plane (inference workers) are pinned to tenancy boundaries. Hosted: logical multitenancy with per-request tenant id, KV cache namespace separation and per-tenant memory isolation enforced at the runtime process level. Private/VPC: network-enforced tenant isolation via subnets and security groups.

2) Egress and gateway model:
   - Egress gateways (customer-configurable for Private) are colocated at the cluster edge; support NAT + logging to customer log sinks. Hosted deployments route egress through our hardened, monitored egress fleet with allowlists and TLS interception for traffic scanning as agreed.

3) KV cache / model state threat note:
   - Threat: adversarial prompt or model-stealing attempts via crafted inputs leading to cross-request leakage.
   - Mitigations: per-tenant KV namespaces, eviction and TTL policies, runtime mem-scrub on model swap, and audit hooks for unusual cache hits. Evidence: runtime config file named runtime/cache_namespaces.yaml (included in security-evidence.zip).

4) Sidecar compromise / lateral movement:
   - Threat: a compromised sidecar attempts to reach other tenant workers or control plane.
   - Mitigations: eBPF-based network policies; strict outbound firewall rules; process-level seccomp profiles; RBAC for sidecar control plane calls; periodic canarying on sidecar images. Evidence: firewall ruleset snippet (included).

5) Recommended snippet for the annotations doc: a short table of risk, likelihood, impact, and primary controls. Keep it high level for customer RFI.

Attaching the preliminary evidence bundle for indexing. I'll refine the threat descriptions once Marcus confirms runtime nuance.

Irene Choi
Senior Security Engineer
Redwood Inference

--
Attachment: security-evidence.zip (zip stub containing: runtime/cache_namespaces.yaml, firewall-rules-snippet.txt, audit-log-sample.json)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Jules Martin <jules.martin@wavefin.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-04-20T15:42:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: Deployment isolation map, egress controls, and high-level threat annotations

Hi Jules — thanks for the patience. Attached is a concise package addressing your items: an annotated deployment map, a short threat-annotation summary, and an evidence index. Key points up front: 

- Isolation: control plane and data plane are segregated; tenant data is isolated at runtime via namespace separation and memory hygiene controls. 
- Egress: Private deployments allow customer-managed gateways; Hosted egress is routed through our hardened fleet and logged to customer-configured sinks.
- Threats: we call out the top risks (cross-tenant leakage, KV cache contamination, sidecar compromise) and the specific mitigations (namespaces, TTLs, seccomp/eBPF network policy, canary image checks).

Deliverables (attached):
- deployment-isolation-map-v1.svg (diagram with legend for control plane, worker pools, sidecars, and egress points)
- threat-annotations-v1.pdf (4 one-page threat summaries with mitigations and evidence pointers)
- security-evidence.zip (index -> runtime/cache_namespaces.yaml, firewall-rules-snippet.txt, audit-log-sample.json)

Relevant links: https://docs.redwood.ai/inf-platform/deployment-isolation/2026-04 (detailed doc)

If helpful, I'd be glad to schedule a 30-minute walkthrough tomorrow. Are you available 10:00–10:30 PT?

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Senior Sales Engineer
Redwood Inference
Phone: +1 (415) 555-0198
--
On Tue, Apr 20, 2026 at 12:03 PM Jules Martin <jules.martin@wavefin.com> wrote:
> Thanks — we received the package and will review. 10:00 PT tomorrow works for a walkthrough.

--
Attachment: deployment-isolation-map-v1.svg (svg stub)
Attachment: threat-annotations-v1.pdf (pdf stub)
Attachment: security-evidence.zip (zip stub)

   - Mitigations: eBPF-based network policies; strict outbound firewall rules; process-level seccomp profiles; RBAC for sidecar control plane calls; periodic canarying on sidecar images. Evidence: firewall ruleset snippet (included).

5) Recommended snippet for the annotations doc: a short table of risk, likelihood, impact, and primary controls. Keep it high level for customer RFI.

Attaching the preliminary evidence bundle for indexing. I'll refine the threat descriptions once Marcus confirms runtime nuance.

Irene Choi
Senior Security Engineer
Redwood Inference

--
Attachment: security-evidence.zip (zip stub containing: runtime/cache_namespaces.yaml, firewall-rules-snippet.txt, audit-log-sample.json)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Jules Martin <jules.martin@wavefin.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-04-20T15:42:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: Deployment isolation map, egress controls, and high-level threat annotations

Hi Jules — thanks for the patience. Attached is a concise package addressing your items: an annotated deployment map, a short threat-annotation summary, and an evidence index. Key points up front: 

- Isolation: control plane and data plane are segregated; tenant data is isolated at runtime via namespace separation and memory hygiene controls. 
- Egress: Private deployments allow customer-managed gateways; Hosted egress is routed through our hardened fleet and logged to customer-configured sinks.
- Threats: we call out the top risks (cross-tenant leakage, KV cache contamination, sidecar compromise) and the specific mitigations (namespaces, TTLs, seccomp/eBPF network policy, canary image checks).

Deliverables (attached):
- deployment-isolation-map-v1.svg (diagram with legend for control plane, worker pools, sidecars, and egress points)
- threat-annotations-v1.pdf (4 one-page threat summaries with mitigations and evidence pointers)
- security-evidence.zip (index -> runtime/cache_namespaces.yaml, firewall-rules-snippet.txt, audit-log-sample.json)

Relevant links: https://docs.redwood.ai/inf-platform/deployment-isolation/2026-04 (detailed doc)

If helpful, I'd be glad to schedule a 30-minute walkthrough tomorrow. Are you available 10:00–10:30 PT?

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Senior Sales Engineer
Redwood Inference
Phone: +1 (415) 555-0198
--

> Thanks — we received the package and will review. 10:00 PT tomorrow works for a walkthrough.

--
